text.title: Feature Flag Types and Lifecycle Core Rules
text.summary: 7 testable rules grounding the feature-flags-types-lifecycle methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check with sourced rationale; the final rule is the skip-this-methodology gate referenced by the decision tree.

rule.statement: Every flag is exactly one of: release, experiment, ops, permission.
rule.rationale: Mixed-kind flags (release+experiment) have ambiguous retirement criteria; one kind = one lifecycle.

rule.statement: release flags retire ≤30 days after the feature ships; default is 14 days at 100% then cleanup PR.
rule.rationale: Release flags become technical debt after global rollout; longer than 30 days is rot.

rule.statement: experiment flags last 1-4 weeks; cleanup is triggered when target sample size is reached and decision recorded.
rule.rationale: Experiments past 4 weeks are no longer experiments — they are products or rotted decisions.

rule.statement: ops/kill-switch flags can live indefinitely but must be reviewed annually for relevance.
rule.rationale: Kill switches are operational tools, not features; deletion criteria differs.

rule.statement: permission flags (entitlements per plan/role) live indefinitely but are owned by product, not engineering.
rule.rationale: Permission flags are business rules; engineering does not auto-cleanup them.

rule.statement: Every flag has an owner (team or person). Unowned flags fail the audit.
rule.rationale: Without an owner, cleanup never happens.

rule.statement: If you have no flags yet or only one trivial flag, skip this methodology.
rule.rationale: Taxonomy requires multiple flags to pay back.
